summary:
Claim: Enlargement of the blind spot results from physical expansion of the optic disc.  
Evidence: - Papilledema causes swelling of the optic disc, increasing its size and expanding the blind spot’s visible boundary. - Glaucoma primarily causes peripheral vision loss, not enlargement of the blind spot itself.  
Answer: D

The claim is that enlargement of the blind spot arises from physical expansion of the optic disc. Key evidence includes papilledema—swelling of the optic disc due to elevated intracranial pressure—which increases the disc's size and thereby expands the blind spot visually. In contrast, glaucoma leads to retinal ganglion cell loss and peripheral vision defect, not blind spot enlargement. Thus, among listed options, only papilledema produces measurable anatomical growth at the optic disc level, directly altering the blind spot’s dimensions. Final answer: D.
